4: mah enosh ki tizkerenu uben adam ki tipqedenu (mâ-ʾĕnôš kî-tizqĕrennû ûben-ʾādām kî tipqĕdennû): 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?' Ben adam (son of man) in Ps 8 is the representative human made lower than the heavenly beings and crowned with glory — a meditation on Genesis 1:26-28's dominion mandate. The NT (Hebrews 2:5-9) applies Ps 8 to Christ as the true human who fulfills the Adamic mandate: 'We do not yet see everything in subjection to him, but we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death.' Christ recapitulates and fulfills humanity's Ps 8 vocation.
1:
יְהוָה אֲדֹנֵינוּ (YHWH ʾădōnênû, "O LORD our Lord") — a compressed theologoumenon: the personal covenant name YHWH combined with the sovereign title ʾādôn (master/lord) with the first-person plural suffix: our sovereign. The doubled address — YHWH as personal name, ʾAdônênû as title — appears identically at the psalm's close (v9), forming an inclusio that brackets the entire meditation.

אֲשֶׁר-תְּנָה הוֹדְךָ עַל-הַשָּׁמָיִם (ʾăsher-tĕnāh hôdĕkā ʿal-hashāmāyim, "You have placed your glory above the heavens") — hôd (splendor/majesty/glory) exceeding even the heavens in scope. The same word used of royal majesty (1 Chronicles 29:25) and the garment of divine splendor (Psalms 104:1). The glory that cannot be contained in the heavens (1 Kings 8:27) is the overflow of the Creator's nature.
2: מִפִּי עוֹלְלִים וְיֹנְקִים (mippî ʿôlĕlîm wĕyōnĕqîm, "From the mouths of children and nursing infants") — ʿôlĕlîm (children, those who glean or play) and yōnĕqîm (nursing infants, from yānaq, to suck): the most dependent and inarticulate of human beings. יִסַּדְתָּ עֹז (yissadtā ʿōz, "you have ordained praise") — the key translation decision: ʿōz means "strength/fortress/might" but the LXX rendered it αἶνον (praise), and that is how Jesus cites it in Matthew 21:16 ("from the mouth of children you have prepared praise"). The MKT follows the LXX interpretation; the Hebrew is genuinely ambiguous — "you have established strength/praise" through the weakest voices.
3: מַעֲשֵׂה אֶצְבְּעֹתֶיךָ (maʿăśê ʾeṣbĕʿōtêkā, "the work of your fingers") — ʾeṣbaʿ (finger): the delicate, precise digit rather than the hand. Psalms 19:1 uses "hands" for the heavens; here Psalms 8:3 uses "fingers" for the moon and stars — the fine-detail work of precise craftsmanship, as if the stars were set in place individually with fingertips. The cosmos as divine handiwork of unimaginable precision and care.
5:
וַתְּחַסְּרֵהוּ מְּעַט מֵאֱלֹהִים (watĕḥassĕrêhû mĕʿaṭ mêʾĕlōhîm, "Yet you have made him just a little lower than the heavenly beings") — the central interpretive crux: is ʾĕlōhîm God himself, or divine/heavenly beings (angels)? All three options have ancient support: the LXX reads ἀγγέλους (angels), which Hebrews 2:7 follows and applies to Christ. If the MT means God, the gap between humanity and the creator is rendered almost infinitesimal — a remarkable statement of human dignity. The MKT "heavenly beings" preserves the ambiguity while leaning toward the LXX/Hebrews reading.

וְכָבוֹד וְהָדָר תְּעַטְּרֵהוּ (wĕkābôd wĕhādār tĕʿaṭṭĕrêhû, "crowned him with glory and honor") — the same royal vocabulary used of the Davidic king (Psalms 21:5) applied to humanity as a whole. The crown (ʿāṭar) is the royal coronation — humanity as the crown-bearing representative of the Creator in the created order.
6: תַּמְשִׁילֵהוּ בְּמַעֲשֵׂי יָדֶיךָ (tamshîlêhû bĕmaʿăśê yādêkā, "You have given him dominion over the works of your hands") — māshal (hiphil: to cause to rule/have dominion); the same verb root that underlies "ruling" in Genesis 1:26-28. Humanity is placed in the position of ruling over what God's hands made — a profound delegation of creative authority. כֹּל שַׁתָּה תַחַת-רַגְלָיו (kōl shattāh taḥat-raglāyw, "you have placed everything under his feet") — "all" (kōl) without qualification; "under his feet" (taḥat raglāyw) is the posture of subjects before a king, enemies before a conqueror. Hebrews 2:8 acknowledges that "we do not yet see everything subjected to him" — the psalm describes the eschatological reality that Christ will fully enact.
7: צֹנֶה וַאֲלָפִים כֻּלָּם (ṣōneh waʾălāpîm kullām, "all sheep and cattle") — the specification of "all sheep and cattle" begins the inventory of the creation under human dominion, descending from domesticated animals to wild, then to the sky, then the sea. The comprehensiveness matches Genesis 1:28's categories. The verse has no syntactic complexity — it is a list, and the simplicity amplifies the sweep of the grant.
8: צִפּוֹר שָׁמַיִם וּדְגֵי הַיָּם (ṣippôr shāmayim ûdĕgê hayyām, "the birds of the sky and the fish of the sea") — the inventory now covers the three realms: land (v7), sky, and sea. עֹבֵר אָרְחוֹת יַמִּים (ʿōbêr ʾorĕḥôt yammîm, "whatever moves along the ocean's currents") — the participle ʿōbêr (moving along) and the plural ʾorĕḥôt (paths/currents) of yammîm (seas): whatever passes through the lanes of the sea. The underwater paths suggest the orderly structure even of ocean currents — the cosmos is not chaotic but has paths. All creatures in all domains are named as subject to the human delegate.
9: The verbal repetition of v1 — יְהוָה אֲדֹנֵינוּ מָה-אַדִּיר שִׁמְךָ בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ — closes the inclusio. The meditation moves from praise of the Name (v1), through the marvel of human dignity within the creation (vv2-8), back to praise of the Name (v9). The theological movement is: the majestic Creator has made frail humans (ʾĕnôsh, v4) as his ruling representatives — a paradox that resolves only in the one who is fully both: the Son of Man who is also Lord of all.
